questions.prompt: It has been several months since phishing response procedures were tested. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Schedule a phishing response exercise with relevant teams

options.label: B
options.text: Quickly reread the policy alone

options.label: C
options.text: Assume staff remember what to do

questions.prompt: Recent phishing attempts were reported internally. How should this be used?

options.label: A
options.text: Share lessons learned and update awareness materials

options.label: B
options.text: File the report for record purposes only

options.label: C
options.text: Ignore since the incident is already closed

questions.prompt: Security bulletins warn of a new phishing campaign targeting your industry. What is the best response?

options.label: A
options.text: Alert staff and remind them what to watch for

options.label: B
options.text: Wait until someone reports a similar email

options.label: C
options.text: Assume filters will block everything

questions.prompt: A new employee asks how to report suspicious emails. What should happen?

options.label: A
options.text: Direct them to the official reporting channel and guidance

options.label: B
options.text: Tell them to ask their manager if it happens

options.label: C
options.text: Say IT will notice automatically

questions.prompt: Phishing training is due for renewal. What approach is most effective?

options.label: A
options.text: Use updated examples and simulate real phishing scenarios

options.label: B
options.text: Send a reminder email about security

options.label: C
options.text: Skip training this year to reduce workload

questions.prompt: An employee forwards a suspicious email. What should the service desk do first?

options.label: A
options.text: Log it as a security incident and escalate

options.label: B
options.text: Tell the employee to delete it and move on

options.label: C
options.text: Ignore unless more people report it

questions.prompt: Multiple users report similar suspicious emails. What should happen?

options.label: A
options.text: Notify and activate the incident response team

options.label: B
options.text: Wait to see if anyone clicks the link

options.label: C
options.text: Let helpdesk handle it alone

questions.prompt: The message appears to be from senior management requesting urgent action. What is important now?

options.label: A
options.text: Verify authenticity before anyone responds

options.label: B
options.text: Assume it’s real due to the sender’s name

options.label: C
options.text: Ask staff to respond quickly just in case

questions.prompt: Reports show some staff opened the email. What should be confirmed?

options.label: A
options.text: Who received it, who clicked, and possible impact

options.label: B
options.text: Only how many emails were sent

options.label: C
options.text: Skip checking and move straight to fixing systems

questions.prompt: The email requested login credentials. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Escalate according to defined phishing severity levels

options.label: B
options.text: Handle informally within the team

options.label: C
options.text: Avoid escalation to prevent alarm

questions.prompt: IT wants to investigate affected accounts. What should staff be told?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep emails and avoid deleting evidence

options.label: B
options.text: Clean inboxes immediately

options.label: C
options.text: Ignore instructions and continue working

questions.prompt: It is unclear whether accounts were compromised. What should happen?

options.label: A
options.text: Involve IT/security specialists immediately

options.label: B
options.text: Ask users to check their own accounts first

options.label: C
options.text: Wait until systems fail

questions.prompt: Some employees clicked the link. What must be confirmed?

options.label: A
options.text: Whether credentials or data were entered

options.label: B
options.text: Only whether the email looked convincing

options.label: C
options.text: Assume nothing happened

questions.prompt: Several departments received the phishing email. What should be assessed?

options.label: A
options.text: Number of users affected and possible data exposure

options.label: B
options.text: Only the first person who reported it

options.label: C
options.text: Treat it as a single-user issue

questions.prompt: The phishing message may still be circulating internally. What should be checked?

options.label: A
options.text: Whether it was forwarded or spread through replies

options.label: B
options.text: Only the original inbox

options.label: C
options.text: Assume the issue stopped already

questions.prompt: One user entered login details on the phishing site. What is critical now?

options.label: A
options.text: Check account access, data exposure, and connected systems

options.label: B
options.text: Reset password only and move on

options.label: C
options.text: Assume no damage occurred

questions.prompt: The compromised account contains customer data. What is safest?

options.label: A
options.text: Escalate to the data protection lead for assessment

options.label: B
options.text: Monitor quietly without informing anyone

options.label: C
options.text: Avoid reporting to reduce concern

questions.prompt: Suspicious login activity is ongoing. What should be done?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediately restrict affected accounts

options.label: B
options.text: Observe activity for a while first

options.label: C
options.text: Ignore to avoid disruption

questions.prompt: A user confirms their password was entered on the phishing site. What now?

options.label: A
options.text: Force password reset and secure the account

options.label: B
options.text: Ask them to change it later

options.label: C
options.text: Do nothing unless misuse appears

questions.prompt: An attachment from the phishing email was opened. What is the safest response?

options.label: A
options.text: Scan and clean affected devices immediately

options.label: B
options.text: Wait to see if problems appear

options.label: C
options.text: Assume antivirus already handled it

questions.prompt: Some machines were taken offline. How should they return to use?

options.label: A
options.text: Restore safely and confirm they are clean

options.label: B
options.text: Put them back online quickly

options.label: C
options.text: Skip checks to save time

questions.prompt: After the incident, what additional step strengthens security?

options.label: A
options.text: Enforce MFA or extra monitoring on affected accounts

options.label: B
options.text: Send a reminder to be careful next time

options.label: C
options.text: Take no additional action

questions.prompt: IT suspects deeper compromise. What approach is most reliable?

options.label: A
options.text: Rebuild devices from trusted sources

options.label: B
options.text: Delete suspicious files only

options.label: C
options.text: Resume use without checks

questions.prompt: Systems appear stable again. What should be done before full resumption?

options.label: A
options.text: Confirm risks are removed and communicate clearance

options.label: B
options.text: Resume operations immediately

options.label: C
options.text: Let each team decide independently

questions.prompt: The phishing incident is resolved. What documentation is needed?

options.label: A
options.text: A structured report with timeline, impact and response

options.label: B
options.text: Short email summary only

options.label: C
options.text: Avoid documentation

questions.prompt: How should the organisation use this incident?

options.label: A
options.text: Review what worked and improve controls/training

options.label: B
options.text: Discuss informally only

options.label: C
options.text: Move on without review

questions.prompt: Employees know something happened. What is best?

options.label: A
options.text: Share guidance to prevent similar phishing attacks

options.label: B
options.text: Tell only managers

options.label: C
options.text: Say nothing to avoid attention

questions.prompt: Weaknesses in the response were found. What now?

options.label: A
options.text: Update procedures and security controls formally

options.label: B
options.text: Make minor notes only

options.label: C
options.text: Leave everything unchanged
